common.location: Rapid rivers, shallow coastal rivers and lakes - Japan, Korea, China and Taiwan
common.fun_fact: The Ayu (Plecoglossus altivelis) is fished from June to September between 09h and 16h, as it activates under sunlight to graze on rocky algae. A surprising biological fact is its territorial combativeness: in Japan, it is caught by attaching a live decoy that triggers an attack from the wild fish defending its territory. Biologists observe that its skin releases a fruity watermelon scent due to volatile aldehydes. Sold around 20 euros per kilo, this fish is a highly prized culinary delicacy when grilled, thanks to its fine, fragrant flesh.

common.location: Lakes, ponds and lazy rivers - North America and Europe
common.fun_fact: This predator is especially active during light transition hours, hunting on sight in the morning and at the end of the day when the prey moves close to the surface to feed. The favorable period extends from late spring to mid-autumn because this thermophilic fish needs warm water to stimulate its metabolism and territorial aggressiveness. One of its most impressive characteristics is its ability to jump out of the water, shaking its head frantically to try to free itself when it feels captured by an intruder. In addition, its enormous mouth allows it to engulf prey measuring up to a third of its own size, including frogs, small birds or unwary rodents crossing seagrass beds.

common.location: Sea, ocean - North-East Atlantic Ocean, English Channel, Mediterranean Sea
common.fun_fact: This predator stalks its prey mainly during the night and high tide, taking advantage of the darkness to surprise small fish directly in the foam of the coastal surf. Its presence near riprap and estuaries extends from spring to late autumn, when the temperature of coastal waters remains mild, before the adults join the deep sea offshore for winter spawning. When handling it, it is imperative to be wary of its lateral gill covers, because these bony structures are real natural razors capable of cutting cleanly a fishing line or a careless finger. This physical danger is accompanied by a formidable fishing intelligence, the large specimens having a developed long-term memory which allows them to recognize and avoid an artificial lure if they have already been captured in the past.

common.location: Rivers, lakes, ponds - Central & Western Europe
common.fun_fact: This benthic fish is mainly active during the nocturnal and twilight hours, taking advantage of the darkness to peacefully search the mud without exposing itself to visual predators. Its foraging peaks in late spring to early fall, when warm waters speed up its metabolism and cause it to feed continuously after spawning. To protect itself from parasites and infections in these stagnant environments, the animal produces a phenomenal quantity of exceptionally thick and viscous protective mucus. Competition fishermen particularly fear large catches of this species because this abundant and tenacious glue instantly engulfs the lines and keepnets, leaving a stubborn smell of sediment and forcing a complete cleaning of the equipment after each session.

common.location: Muddy ponds, shallow lakes, canals and slow-moving rivers - Europe and Asia
common.fun_fact: This benthic cyprinid searches for food mainly during the day, active from morning until dusk to search the mud using its protractile mouth when the light allows small invertebrates to be spotted. Its capture is ideal from the end of spring to the beginning of autumn, a period during which the stagnant waters warm up and stimulate its metabolism as well as its reproductive gatherings in the seagrass beds. This fish has an absolutely extraordinary biological adaptation allowing it to survive several weeks in waters completely deprived of oxygen, or even frozen by ice in winter. To achieve this feat, its body switches to a unique anaerobic metabolism which transforms glycogen into ethanol, a form of pure alcohol then evacuated through the gills, thus preventing its cells from freezing or asphyxiating when the pond transforms into a solid block.

common.location: Lakes, ponds, canals and slow-moving rivers - Europe and Asia
common.fun_fact: This massive cyprinid feeds mainly during twilight and at night, when calm returns to the body of water and it can search the sediment in complete safety. The period from May to October corresponds to the warm waters necessary to activate the metabolism of this thermophilic fish, stimulating its feeding frenzy after the spring spawning. One of its astonishing abilities lies in its extraordinary sense of taste: its four barbels and its lips are lined with thousands of chemical receptors allowing it to detect nutrients buried under several centimeters of mud without seeing anything. A striking observation on large specimens shows that they have extremely sensitive hearing capable of memorizing the vibrations of footsteps on the bank or lead smacking on the water, pushing them to instantly flee the area to take refuge offshore.

common.location: Rapid rivers, streams, reservoirs - Europe
common.fun_fact: This opportunistic diurnal hunter is fully active under sunlight to spot its prey on the surface thanks to its keen eyesight, while the months of May to September correspond to the hot season when its metabolism is maximum, pushing it to actively maraud under riparian vegetation. This cyprinid is distinguished by its absolutely extraordinary omnivorous diet, capable of devouring with the same voracity cherries or wild blackberries that have fallen from riverside trees as pieces of strong-smelling cheese. Nicknamed the river devil, it nevertheless has a very complex culinary profile: although its white flesh is fine and firm, its skeleton incorporates a particularly dense network of Y-shaped branched ridges, making its consumption therefore laborious at the table.

common.location: Sea, ocean - North-East Atlantic Ocean, English Channel, Mediterranean Sea, North Sea
common.fun_fact: This pelagic fish migrates vertically to hunt under cover of darkness, following plankton and schools of anchovies which rise to the surface during the night. Catches are optimal from late spring to early autumn because the warming of the waters pushes the large schools to move closer to the coasts and surface layers. During their youth, juveniles use a fascinating survival strategy by taking refuge among the venomous tentacles of large jellyfish, taking advantage of skin immunity to escape predators without suffering stings. Although often shunned in Europe where it is relegated to the rank of fishing by-product due to its line of sharp scales, it is a real star of gastronomy in Japan under the name of Aji, where certain sedentary coastal populations having never migrated develop such an exceptional golden color and fat content that they fetch astronomical prices on the Tokyo markets.

common.location: Shallow ponds, grassy lakes, canals and calm rivers - North America (and introduced to Europe)
common.fun_fact: This diurnal predator operates exclusively in bright light, hunting on sight in the heart of shallow seagrass beds during the brightest hours of the day. The months of May to September correspond to its favorite period because this thermophilic fish requires well-warmed waters to actively feed and carry out its coastal reproduction. During this phase, the male builds a very neat circular nest by sweeping the sand with his tail, then defends his eggs with fierce aggression, not hesitating to attack intruders three times his size or to bite the fingers of bathers. Aquarists know it well for its exacerbated territoriality and its ability to recognize humans who approach to feed it, radically changing color depending on their state of excitement in front of the glass.

common.location: Rivers, streams, lakes and ponds - Europe and North America
common.fun_fact: The Crayfish (Procambarus clarkii) is caught from April to September all day long, taking advantage of warm waters to grow and moult. An amazing biological feature is its autotomy power: it can abandon a claw to escape a predator, a limb that then regrows. Naturalists observe that its antennules detect chemical food signals from a great distance. Sold for about 12 euros per kilo, this crustacean is famous for its taste value, preparing divinely in bisque or simply poached in court-bouillon.

common.location: Estuaries, shallow bays, freshwater marshes and coastal lakes - North America (Eastern Canada and Northeastern United States)
common.fun_fact: This small fish is active mainly during the sunny hours of the day, cruising in tight schools just below the water's surface to hunt tiny invertebrates on sight with its upward-facing mouth. The season extending from May to September corresponds to the period when the waters of the banks warm up, favoring its reproduction in the coastal aquatic vegetation. This species has a remarkable physiological tolerance allowing it to move instantly from the purest fresh water to the highly brackish waters of maritime estuaries without undergoing osmotic shock. Faced with an aerial or aquatic predator, this tiny fish deploys a surprising escape strategy by suddenly diving to the bottom to sink and hide entirely in the mud or sand, leaving only its clear eyes visible.

common.location: Northeast Atlantic Ocean, North Sea, English Channel and Baltic Sea
common.fun_fact: The Dab (Limanda limanda) is fished from October to April all day long, when it approaches shallow coastal waters to feed. A surprising biological fact is its rough skin like sandpaper due to tiny scales pointing towards the tail. Sailors notice on the field its lateral line which draws a characteristic arched curve above its pectoral fin. Sold for about 15 euros per kilo, this flatfish has a delicate white flesh that is excellent pan-seared with a lemon butter.

common.location: Muddy ponds, swamps, backwaters and very slow rivers - Europe
common.fun_fact: This benthic cobitid is active exclusively during the night to search the sediments, remaining buried in the mud throughout the day to protect itself from light and predators. Its spring capture period corresponds to its biological awakening and its reproduction phase, during which individuals emerge more frequently from deep areas to regroup in shallow seagrass beds. A remarkable biological ability allows it to swallow air at the surface and assimilate it through its intestinal walls in the event of oxygen deficiency, which causes a hissing or slight gaseous noise when handled out of water. Furthermore, its great sensitivity to variations in atmospheric pressure makes it a natural barometer: frenetic agitation, characterized by erratic swimming towards the surface, almost systematically announces the arrival of a storm or an imminent meteorological disturbance.

common.location: lake, pond, river; North America (Canada, United States)
common.fun_fact: Active from 6 a.m. to 8 p.m., this diurnal fish feeds mainly by sight. Its period of high activity extends from May to August (05 - 08) to optimize its metabolism in temperate waters. Amazingly, its eggs are laid in long, unique gelatinous ribbons that can be up to two meters long. In the field, biologists often observe dense schools of juveniles taking refuge in coastal vegetation to escape the dazzling attacks of the northern pike.

common.location: Lake, pond, slow-moving river (North America, Europe)
common.fun_fact: Lepomis gibbosus is active from 6 a.m. - 8 p.m. because this predator hunts exclusively by sight. Its reproduction extends from May - August and 05 - 08 in warm waters. Surprisingly, the male builds and fiercely defends a circular nest, cleaning the substrate. During my field observations, a male aggressively charged my shadow repeatedly. Its flesh is edible, white and firm, but it is rarely cooked due to its small size and the profusion of bones.

common.location: Lake, pond, slow river (Europe, Western Asia)

From May to August (05 - 08), rising temperatures stimulate its reproduction in dense vegetation. This animal tolerates strong organic pollution and prolonged absence of oxygen. Observing the schools brushing against the water lilies, you can see its bright red fins. Its white flesh has a fine flavor despite many annoying ridges.

common.location: North-East Atlantic Ocean (from Iceland to Senegal), Mediterranean Sea, Black Sea. Coastal pelagic environment.
common.fun_fact: Sardina pilchardus migrates vertically from 8 p.m. to 4 a.m. to feed on the surface under cover of darkness. Its optimal capture extends from May to August, the season when it accumulates fat reserves. This clupeid communicates in a surprising way via gas expulsions creating sound bubbles to maintain the cohesion of the school. At sea, the silvery glow on its sides goes out instantly after capture. Its fatty flesh, rich in omega-3, is ideal grilled on the barbecue.

common.location: North Atlantic Ocean, North Sea, English Channel, Baltic Sea, rocky coastal areas and kelp beds
common.fun_fact: The Liparis liparis is active from 9 p.m. - 4 a.m. to stalk small benthic crustaceans under the cover of darkness. Its presence in the intertidal zone peaks from around November - March, during its breeding period in cold waters. This fish has a ventral sucker disc resulting from the modification of its pelvic fins to adhere to rocks beaten by waves. Biologists often observe it attached under algae. Its gelatinous and bland flesh limits its culinary use to local soups.

common.location: Rivers, estuaries and coastal areas of Northern and Western Europe (North Sea, Baltic Sea, Atlantic Ocean)
common.fun_fact: Osmerus eperlanus is active from 8 p.m. - 4 a.m. to escape predators on the surface and migrates from March to May upstream of the estuaries to spawn but it can also be found from November onwards. This fish emits a striking smell of fresh cucumber due to a volatile molecule, trans-2-nonenal. On the ground, we spot the banks with this characteristic scent hovering above the water. In cooking, its fine and delicate flesh is traditionally eaten whole, fried and crispy, after a simple dip in flour.

Caught from March to November, especially between 19h and 04h in rainy, overcast weather, it navigates the dark using eight sensory barbels. Watch out for its sharp, venomous spines. Priced at 90 gold coins, its rich, oily flesh is highly praised in spicy stews or deep-fried dishes.

Caught from June to September, especially between 20h and 04h on dark nights in coastal estuaries, it hunts actively for small fish. Its silver scales shed easily during fights. Priced at 470 gold coins, its fine, pinkish flesh is a delicacy when pan-seared or gently poached in a seasoned court-bouillon.

Caught from April to November, especially from 06h to 10h under calm waters, it roams Mediterranean sandy bays to crush shells with strong teeth. It is a protandrous hermaphrodite. Valued at 110 gold coins, its firm white flesh is highly prized when grilled with fennel or baked in a salt crust.

Caught from June to October, mainly between 08h and 16h in sunny weather, it feeds on worms and small crustaceans on Mediterranean sandy bottoms. Priced at 360 gold coins, its delicate white flesh is excellent when baked whole in foil or roasted with lemon.

Valued at 260 gold coins, it represents a pinnacle of marine gastronomy, offering firm, succulent flesh perfect when poached in champagne.

Fished from May to October, especially between 08h and 17h in clear water, it uses strong teeth to feed on mollusks. They are protogynous hermaphrodites, changing from female to male. Valued at 270 gold coins, its soft flesh is highly suited for traditional, slow-simmered fish soups.

Fished from May to September, mainly between 06h and 10h during strong tides, it darts from cover to swallow sandeels. It features a prominent lower jaw. Priced at 370 gold coins, its delicate, flaky white flesh is best eaten very fresh, either baked in the oven or gently poached.

Caught from March to December, especially between 08h and 18h in choppy seas, it forms dense schools around submerged wrecks. It features a unique chin barbel. Priced at 80 gold coins, its tender but delicate flesh must be eaten very fresh, making it perfect for quick pan-frying or poaching in milk.

Caught year-round, ideally between 08h and 18h on sunny days, it hides under stones due to its lack of a swim bladder. It is recognized by a wide, flattened head. Priced at 70 gold coins, its fine white flesh is a rare culinary delicacy, traditionally fried whole as a crispy appetizer.

Priced at 150 gold coins, its oily, flavorful flesh is highly popular when grilled whole, smoked in oil, or prepared similarly to anchovies.

uncommon.fun_fact: Engraulis encrasicolus exhibits essentially diurnal feeding activity, grouping together in dense schools at depth during the day to escape predators. Targeted fishing exploits these vertical migration cycles, with schools often dispersing at dusk. Surprisingly, its lifestyle is extremely plastic, switching from filtration to selective collection depending on the density of prey. Cooked fresh, it offers iodized and fine flesh, very popular when fried or marinated, far from the excess salt of industrial preserves.

uncommon.fun_fact: The common barbel, Barbus barbus, is ideally fished from May to October, between 6 p.m. and 6 a.m., when its nocturnal benthic activity is in full swing for feeding. This cyprinid has a surprising adaptation: its four fleshy barbels are saturated with chemical receptors allowing it to detect its prey in the total darkness of the sediments. In the field, an expert will notice that its eggs are highly toxic to humans during spawning, causing serious enteritis. Sold for around 5 euros per kilo, its firm flesh but full of bones is traditionally prepared in a terrine or court-bouillon.

uncommon.location: Slow-moving rivers, canals, ponds, backwaters and lakes rich in vegetation and sediment. Present in Europe (from eastern France to the Urals) and in Asia Minor.
uncommon.fun_fact: The European cowherd (Rhodeus amarus) is fished from April to August, during its reproduction, because the males then display splendid spawning colors to attract females. This fish maintains an obligatory symbiotic relationship with freshwater mussels (such as anodonts). The female develops an ovipositor several centimeters long to lay her eggs directly in the gills of the bivalve. Sold for around five euros in aquarium shops, this fish is protected in France. It has a bitter and very rough flesh which makes it completely unsuitable for culinary consumption.

Sold for around 17 euros per kilo to wholesalers, it is highly sought after in gastronomy. Its firm, iodized flesh becomes incredibly tender when quickly seared on the grill or simmered in ink.

Observing the specimen in the field, we notice that its eyes both migrate to the left side of its head during its juvenile metamorphosis. Marketed at around 15 euros per kilo, its white, fine and firm flesh is ideal for poached fillets.

Not marketed for human consumption, its gelatinous texture and micro-size make it completely unsuitable for culinary or gastronomic use.

Sold around 12 euros per kilo, this esteemed fish offers a very fine white flesh ideal for baking whole.

Its very fine flesh is consumed deep-fried whole in the mountainous regions of Asia.

Experts observe that its large red scales are so hard they resist piranha bites. Sold for 15000 gold coins, its boneless, firm, and flavorful white flesh is nicknamed the cod of the Amazon and is a highly sought-after delicacy.

Ichthyologists observe that the male incubates the eggs in its mouth to protect its offspring. Sold for 15500 gold coins, its immense ornamental value and status as a lucky charm in Asia protect it from consumption.

Estimated at 13000 gold coins, this fish is a culinary wonder, prized for its white, firm, and flavorful grilled flesh.

Sold for 16000 gold coins, its firm white flesh is eaten fried, but requires careful preparation to remove the deadly eggs.

Sold for 16500 gold coins, its translucent white flesh cut in paper-thin slices is the base of Japanese fugu.

Estimated at 14000 gold coins, its gelatinous flesh has no culinary value.

legendary.fun_fact: The Sawfish (Pristis pristis) is fished from June to September between 16h and 09h in estuaries and salty lagoons. This cartilaginous fish has a long flat snout bordered with sharp teeth in the shape of a saw. A surprising biological fact is that this snout serves as an electro-sensitive detector and weapon to slash fish. Experts observe that it stirs the sediment with its saw to flush out crustaceans. Sold for 38000 gold coins, this fish is protected and its meat is toxic.

legendary.fun_fact: The Whale Shark (Rhincodon typus) is fished from June to September all day long in warm ocean waters. It is the largest living fish in the world, reaching up to eighteen meters in length. A surprising fact is that this peaceful giant feeds exclusively on plankton by filtering thousands of liters of water per hour. Biologists note that its white spots form a unique pattern for each individual. Sold for 40000 gold coins, its flesh is protected globally and fishing is banned.

legendary.fun_fact: The Golden Trout (Oncorhynchus mykiss aguabonita) is fished from March to May and September to November from 16h to 09h in cliff streams. This altitude jewel surprises with its flamboyant red and golden coat, unique among trout. Mountain guides observe that it only survives in the purest and ice-cold waters of the summits. Estimated at 30000 gold coins, this fish has an incomparably fine pink flesh, delicious simply pan-seared with almonds.
